# Before the day {#sec-before}

Most of what makes a hands-on workshop go smoothly happens before anyone walks in. This is the pre-flight checklist, grouped by how far ahead each item sits. It pulls together the tasks introduced in earlier chapters (setup in Part 2, the iteration org and website in Part 3) into the order you actually do them.

## One to two weeks before

- [ ] Send the pre-course work (@sec-learner-setup) with a clear due date a few days before the workshop, following the timeline in @sec-create-orgs.
- [ ] Open the pre-course survey and share the link. Use it to collect GitHub usernames and to gauge prior experience.
- [ ] Confirm the room, projector, power, and reliable internet (@sec-teacher-setup).
- [ ] Confirm the equipment for live teaching: a flipchart or whiteboard with markers for the live drawings (@sec-live-drawings), a pointer or clicker for advancing slides, and ideally a second screen so your laptop, the projector, and your instructor notes are all visible at once.
- [ ] Confirm catering or breaks for the four-hour session if your venue needs it arranged ahead.

## The pre-course survey

The survey does double duty: it tells you who your learners are, and it surfaces who has not finished the setup. Set it up in **KoboToolbox** (<https://www.kobotoolbox.org>): it is open-source, keeps the data under your control, and exports cleanly to a table you can feed to the `ghclass` invitation scripts (@sec-create-orgs). If you would rather trade the privacy story for the lowest possible friction, Google Forms is a workable alternative; the questions are the same.

At a minimum, ask for:

- [ ] GitHub username (you need this to send organization invitations).
- [ ] Operating system (so you can anticipate install issues).
- [ ] A short self-rating of comfort with R and RStudio.
- [ ] Whether they completed the pre-work, with a space to describe where they got stuck.

::: {.callout-tip}
Build the survey once as a KoboToolbox form you can clone for each cohort. Keep the GitHub-username field as a required short-text question, since it is the one answer the `ghclass` scripts depend on.
:::

## A few days before

- [ ] Invite every learner to the iteration org using the usernames from the survey, via the `ghclass` scripts (@sec-create-orgs).
- [ ] Check who has accepted invitations and made their first commit. Follow up with anyone who has not, in the chat channel.
- [ ] Hold a short virtual office hour or offer chat support for setup problems, so they are solved before the day, not during it.

## Dry-run as the test-learner

This is the highest-value pre-flight step. Using your test-learner account (@sec-teacher-setup), walk the entire workshop as a student would, the day before. The example account used throughout this handbook is the test learner `rainbow-train` (<https://github.com/rainbow-train>), the test profile of the course author; use your own equivalent. Walk through:

- [ ] Clone the material repositories fresh, as the test-learner.
- [ ] Complete every hands-on task in the run of show (@sec-run-of-show) in order.
- [ ] Open and merge a pull request end to end.
- [ ] Note any instruction that was unclear or any step that failed, and fix it before the day.

If you can complete the whole workshop from a clean learner account, the material is ready.

## The morning of

- [ ] Arrive early and confirm the projector, internet, and power work in the actual room.
- [ ] Log in to your instructor account in one browser and your test-learner account in a second, in Chrome (@sec-teacher-setup).
- [ ] Open the cohort website and the slides, ready to demonstrate from the point-and-click interface.
- [ ] Open the chat channel on a device you can monitor during the session.
- [ ] Set up the flipchart or whiteboard and markers for the live drawings (@sec-live-drawings), and check your pointer or clicker works.

## A short triage plan for setup problems

Some learners will still arrive with broken setups. Decide in advance how you will handle it so it does not derail the room:

- [ ] Identify who is not set up in the first few minutes (a quick "make a commit now" check).
- [ ] Pair them with a neighbor who is ready, or seat them near you.
- [ ] Keep moving for the rest of the room; do not hold the whole session for one setup.

The real fix for setup problems is before the day, not during it. Pre-work that is genuinely broken should be caught and resolved through the survey and follow-up (above), so that the morning-of triage is a safety net for a handful of edge cases, not a plan for the whole room.
